About Me
Ayesha Zahid  ·  Brand Strategist (Aspiring)  ·  Founder, Ayesha Socials

I've always been fascinated by people.

Why do we remember some things for years while forgetting others within minutes?

Why does one coffee shop become part of someone's routine while another quietly disappears?

Why does an old phone slowly lose the value it once had?

Those questions have followed me for as long as I can remember.

The difference

Today, I've found a place for them.

It's called Ayesha Socials.

Through observations, psychology, and storytelling, I explore why people connect with certain brands — and how businesses can become memorable. Not by being louder, but by becoming meaningful.

The belief
The best branding lessons aren't always found in marketing books.

Sometimes they're hidden in a coffee shop, an old phone sitting in a drawer, a piece of architecture, or an everyday moment most people walk past.

That's where I start.

Because every memorable brand begins with an observation.

A Ayesha Socials Observations turned into strategy.
The Approach

Most accounts don't need more content. They need better strategy.

Before I write a single caption, every account goes through the same three questions — the same framework I use for client audits and the same one I teach in my own content.

01

Who is this for?

If the answer is "everyone," that's already the problem. Specific content attracts specific customers — speak to one person, not a crowd.

02

What problem does it solve?

People don't save posts because they're beautiful. They save them because they solve something. Every post earns its place by answering one question.

03

What happens next?

Follow, comment, visit, DM — every post needs one specific goal. Without it, even great content has nowhere to send the viewer.

Case Studies

Observations, turned into marketing insight.

None of these are really about coffee, phones, or cookies. Each one starts with something I noticed in everyday life — then traces it back to the psychology and strategy underneath. That's the lens I bring to every account I manage.

"

My approach to content is built on two things: storytelling and psychological marketing. I don't start with "what should I post" — I start with an observation about human behavior, then work backward to the marketing principle hiding inside it. The audience discovers the lesson themselves. That's what makes it stick.

01
Brand Strategy · Emotional Branding

What Starbucks taught me about branding

Carousel Storytelling
+
Observation

People choose Starbucks even when they admit better coffee exists elsewhere.

Problem

Businesses assume a better product alone creates more customers.

Insight

People don't become loyal to products. They become loyal to feelings.

Solution

Used Starbucks as a familiar observation to teach emotional branding — without stating the lesson directly.

Observation

I noticed that many people choose Starbucks even when they admit they can find coffee of similar or even better taste elsewhere. The conversation rarely revolves around the coffee itself. Instead, people talk about the experience, the atmosphere, the cup, their name on the cup, studying there, working there, or simply "going to Starbucks." That made me ask: what are people actually buying?

Marketing problem

Many businesses focus almost entirely on improving their product. They believe a better product automatically creates more customers. But consumers often make purchasing decisions based on emotions and experiences rather than product features alone.

Psychological insight

People don't become loyal to products. They become loyal to feelings. Starbucks transformed coffee into an emotional experience — the product became secondary, the experience became memorable.

Marketing principle

Brand positioning Emotional branding Customer experience Consumer psychology Brand loyalty

My creative solution

Instead of creating another "branding tips" carousel, I used Starbucks as a familiar observation. The story gradually leads readers to discover that memorable brands create emotional experiences rather than simply selling products — the lesson isn't directly stated, the audience discovers it themselves.

Skills demonstrated

Storytelling Consumer psychology Brand strategy Copywriting Carousel design Content marketing
02
Brand Strategy · Brand Evolution

An old Lenovo phone, and why brands get forgotten

Personal observation Storytelling
+
Observation

A once-prized family phone was forgotten in a drawer, then broke — and nobody reacted.

Problem

Businesses slowly become invisible — not because they get worse, but because competitors evolve.

Insight

Familiarity becomes normal. Normal becomes expected. Expected becomes overlooked.

Solution

Used a childhood memory instead of marketing jargon to make the lesson unforgettable.

Observation

While cleaning, I came across an old Lenovo phone that had once been the most valuable device in my family's home. Years later, after newer devices entered our lives, it was forgotten inside a drawer. Eventually it broke. Nobody reacted. Nobody rushed to save it. Its value hadn't disappeared overnight — our perception had changed.

Marketing problem

Many businesses slowly become invisible. Not because they become worse, but because competitors evolve while they remain the same.

Psychological insight

People naturally become familiar with brands. Familiarity eventually becomes normal. Normal becomes expected. Expected becomes overlooked. Brands lose attention gradually — not suddenly.

Marketing principle

Brand repositioning Brand evolution Customer perception Product life cycle Market competition

My creative solution

Instead of discussing product life cycles through marketing jargon, I used a childhood memory. The emotional connection makes the lesson unforgettable: a successful brand continuously reinvents itself before customers stop noticing it.

Skills demonstrated

Emotional storytelling Brand strategy Consumer behavior Psychological marketing Creative writing Visual storytelling
03
Client Audit · Content Strategy

hinova.designs — when talent isn't the problem

Client project Full audit
+
H
hinova.designs — Instagram Profile Audit
Strategic audit · Bio, grid & content pillars
↑
Authority pillar

Case studies, Upwork success, and client results — turned into a permanent pinned post.

↻
Process pillar

Behind-the-scenes content showing how a messy document becomes a polished, sellable asset.

＋
Value pillar

Design tips and common mistakes — content that earns saves and shares from the right audience.

Observation

The artist's work was exceptional, but the page generated limited engagement.

Problem

Talented businesses assume quality alone creates visibility.

Insight

People connect with stories before they connect with products.

Solution

A full audit of messaging, storytelling, and visual communication — not just aesthetics.

Observation

I noticed that the artist produced exceptional work. The craftsmanship was impressive. However, the page generated limited engagement. The issue wasn't artistic ability — it was communication.

Marketing problem

Many talented businesses assume quality alone creates visibility. In reality, customers first need a reason to stop scrolling. Only then can they appreciate the work.

Psychological insight

People don't connect with products first. They connect with stories. Emotion creates attention. Attention creates engagement. Engagement creates trust.

Marketing principle

Content strategy Brand messaging Visual hierarchy Audience psychology Engagement strategy

My creative solution

I performed a strategic audit identifying opportunities to improve messaging, storytelling, visual communication, and audience connection — instead of recommending only aesthetic improvements.

Skills demonstrated

Brand audit UX thinking Content analysis Audience strategy Communication

Live audit document

The full audit was delivered as a Notion document — covering pain points, repositioning strategy, typography overhaul, content pillars, and a pin strategy for authority building.

04
Brand Strategy · Positioning · Scarcity Marketing

Crumbl — how a cookie brand became impossible to ignore

Brand strategy Storytelling
+
Observation

Thousands of bakeries sell cookies. Yet when people think about cookies today, Crumbl enters the conversation.

Challenge

A saturated market where every bakery promised the same thing — fresh, homemade, premium, delicious.

Psychology

They didn't ask "how do we get customers to buy?" They asked "how do we give customers a reason to return?"

Positioning

They didn't own the word "cookies." They owned the feeling of — I wonder what's next.

The observation

Thousands of bakeries sell cookies. Some bake better. Some charge less. Some have existed for decades. Yet somehow, when people think about cookies today, Crumbl enters the conversation. That made me wonder — how does a brand become memorable in a market where almost everyone sells the same product?

The challenge

The bakery industry wasn't waiting for another cookie shop. It was already saturated. Every bakery promised fresh cookies, homemade recipes, premium ingredients, delicious taste — different words, the same positioning. When everyone sounds alike, nobody becomes memorable.

The shift

Crumbl didn't try to convince people it baked the best cookies. Instead, it built an experience people looked forward to. Every week introduced something new — limited-time flavors, a recognizable pink box, a reason to come back. Instead of competing for taste, Crumbl competed for attention.

The psychology

Most businesses ask: "How can we make customers buy from us?" Crumbl asked something different: "How can we give customers a reason to return?" That small shift changed everything. People didn't just buy cookies — they anticipated launches, shared their favorites, compared flavors, created conversations. The product became an experience.

The positioning

Crumbl didn't own the word cookies. It owned the feeling of "I wonder what's next." That's positioning — not becoming better than everyone, but becoming remembered differently.

Marketing principles

Scarcity marketing Customer retention Product launch strategy Brand consistency Consumer psychology Community marketing

Key takeaways

Competing on price creates comparisons Competing on experience creates loyalty Products satisfy needs Positioning creates memories People remember brands that give them something to anticipate

Final insight

The strongest brands don't always create better products. They create a place in people's minds that nobody else owns. That's what positioning is.

Skills demonstrated

Marketing psychology Brand strategy Consumer behavior Storytelling Educational content
05
Content Strategy · Carousel

"Stop posting" — a 3-question filter

Carousel
+

Why I made this

This one isn't an observation case study — it's the framework itself, turned into a carousel. Most businesses don't need more content, they need a filter for the content they already have. Before any post goes out, it has to answer three questions: who is this for, what problem does it solve, and what should the viewer do next. If it can't answer those, it doesn't get posted.

Observation
Marketing lesson
Starbucks
Emotional branding & customer experience
Lenovo
Brand evolution & staying relevant
hinova.designs
Storytelling builds engagement
Crumbl
Scarcity and anticipation drive demand
How I Work

From audit to execution, in four steps.

Step 01

Audit

I review the current profile, bio, grid, and content against the brand's actual goals — not vanity metrics.

Step 02

Position

I define who the content is for, what problem it solves, and the one action every post should drive.

Step 03

Create

Content gets built around that positioning — carousels, captions, and visuals with one clear job each.

Step 04

Refine

I track what's working, drop what isn't, and keep tightening the strategy as the account grows.
